content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I need to get off my phone and watch movies again. Recommend me something, if it gets 10 upvotes, I’ll watch it and reply with what I thought of it.

**Score:** 147
**Body:** I feel like I haven’t watched anything good lately, most of the time I’m half-watching some trash movie or TV shows while scrolling on my phone. I need some strong recommendations. I’m open to pretty much anything.

Drop your best pick and I’ll give it a try, then come back with my feedback.

**Edit:**

Holy s\*\*t, I wasn’t expecting so many of you guys to give me movie suggestions. I am a man of my word though, and I’ll be watching every single movie that gets 10 upvotes or more. I’ll be watching at leas one movie a day until the list is completed, and I’ll reply to your comment before I watch the movie and again afterward with my thoughts on it.

The order in which I’ll be watching the movies will be decided by the number of upvotes, so I’ll go from the most upvoted comment to the least. So stay tuned and be patient, the list is now in the mind-blowing range of 100+ movies, but your turn will come, I promise.

**Movies watched & reviews:**  
\- Children of men  
\- Office Space

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Best In Show
2. Tombstone 
3. Office Space 
4. Jackie Brown
5. Harold & Maude
6. Blade Runner.  Sci-fi isn’t usually my fav but this was good.

Goodfellas is always entertaining
7. Children of men
8. My Cousin Vinny
9. The Thing
10. Jaws

## 2. Looking for Excellent Mystery Thriller Movies. Have watched most of Reddit's Recommendations.

**Score:** 31
**Body:** I have watched most  movies frequently recommended on Reddit. I explicitly want only mystery thrillers(including Sci-fi). No action thrillers(bourne, MI etc) or Comedy thrillers(Seven psychos, Nice guys etc).  Also no Horror/Supernatural elements.  Also no slasher type movies. Era - 1970's and above. Please recommend .Thanks


i also love movies like Hateful eight, no country, Pulp fiction. Tension is built with dialogues. No intense action scenes.


### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Zodiac

Angel Heart

Prisoners

Mystic River

Boston Strangler

The Ninth Gate

Rear Window

Identity

Fallen (1998)

The Pale Blue Eye

The Prestige

The Last Seduction

A Perfect Murder
2. Hopscotch

Blow Out

Dressed to Kill
3. Hmm... you have seem to watch tons of movies already - so I will stay away from the obvious ones first.

The ones left would be foreign film... but the good ones are also well known... so here is my try:

\- Nine Queens (Argentina)

\- Infernal Affair (Hong Kong) which you likely watched.... if then about  The Longest Nite; Project Guttenberg; Cold War.

\- New World (Korea); Perfect Number - remake of Suspect X - though I like Perfect Number better.

\- Sleep Tight (Spain); The Body; The Hidden Face; Department Q movies; The Invisible Guest (Spain)

\- Table 21 (India) 

Now for the obvious one - No Way Out; Mr Brooks; Body Heat;
4. Arlington Road  a great movie
5. It's not really my genre but I have enjoyed a few mystery thrillers. But they aren't obscure so you've probably seen them. 

The Fugitive (1993) 

Memento (2000)

Wanted (2008)

Fallen (1998)

A Time To Kill (1996)

12 Monkeys (1995)

Backdraft (1991)

Fight Club (1999)
6. The talented Mr Ripley 

The Invisible Guest (Spanish)

Black butterfly 

I see you 

The Vanished (  Anne Heche)

The secret in their eyes
7. Get Out can be considered more a social thriller than a horror.
8. Mystic River 
9. Tell No One (French: Ne le dis à personne), 2006, directed by Guillaume Canet
10. Unsane, Vengeance and side effects are three I rarely hear much about but hold in high regard.

## 3. Looking for decent non-american disaster movies

**Score:** 29
**Body:** Looking for suggestions for non-american disaster movies, not interested in slow-paced drama-focused ones but ones with decent scenes of destruction. Some I watched and liked:

The Wave (Bølgen, 2015, Norway) - Tsunami in a fjord.

The Quake (Skjelvet, 2018, Norway) - Earthquake in Oslo, sequel of The Wave.

The Burning Sea (Nordsjøen, 2021, Norway) - Fire in a oil field in the sea.

The Tower (Ta-weo, 2012, South Korea) - Fire in a skyscrapper (style The Towering Inferno).

Ashfall (Baekdusan, 2019, South Korea) - Volcano.

Skyfire (Tian · Huo, 2019, China) - Volcano in a island.  
\----------------------------------------

Edit: i'm interested in movies where the destruction scenes happen on-screen, not after the end ones or those the disaster hits in the last seconds of the movie without really showing anything, like an asteroid movie showing only a flash of light and the end...)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Survival family. Japanese film about society losing electricity.
2. The Quiet Earth(1985) New Zealand made end of the world drama with a famous ending
3. Train to Busan
4. *The Impossible* \- A British family struggles to reunite after the 2004 Boxing Day tsunami in Indonesia.
5. Melancholia (2011) but it is slower paced and the world destruction is not the main focus, but the world destruction is among the best you’ll see
6. Troll (2022) - there is a sequel (Troll 2) coming out December 1st I believe..
7. Pompeii?
8. Exit (Korea, 2019)
9. The Wandering Earth Project.  There's a sequel too, but i havent seen it.
10. I just watched Godzilla Minus One last night, not a natural disaster movie but good ‘ol Godzilla does a number on Tokyo

## 4. Looking for "Americana" themed movies. Ones that show the mundane aspects of American life

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Been fascinated by American town culture for a long time now. I really love the aesthetics of it, the diners, the "small town" vibe, the stories etc. Things that are purely American/Western. A good example was Forks in the first Twilight movie. Obv there's a story set in such towns but I really need that American vibe

I'm open to any genre though I prefer horror/mystery. Open to shows too, my all time favourite is Supernatural S1-5

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. American Graffiti is a nice dive into car culture of the 50s.
2. what's eating gilbert grape
3. Napoleon Dynamite will give you a taste of life in small town Idaho. Gosh.
4. No Country for Old Men
5. Fargo
6. The Last Picture Show

Tremors

Roadhouse
7. If you literally just want to snapshot of Americana, look at "Vernon, Florida" it's literally a documentary interviewing for five people from Vernon in 1981. Plot? None.  Engaging, 100%
8. Paper Moon for Depression-era life.
9. The Straight Story
10. Mermaids with Winona Ryder and baby Christina Ricci has some good small town vibes 

## 5. Movies with a character who has Dissociative Identity Disorder (DID)

**Score:** 21
**Body:** I am looking for movies where the main character or someone else has Dissociative Identity Disorder (DID). For example: Psycho (1960), Perfect Blue (1997), Split (2016).

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Identity
2. I feel like this whole thread is one big spoiler but Hide and Seek (2005)

If you’re allowing TV, The United States of Tara is a phenomenal show.
  - Another vote for United States of Tara!
3. *Spoiler alert*



The secret window
  - I went into this movie blind thinking “a Jonny Depp movie, I’ll give it a try,” and I was not disappointed in the best way.
4. Sybill
5. The Voices (2014)
6. Fight Club
7. The Three Faces of Eve. Great movie.
8. I have plenty of great ones to recommend but just mentioning they are about DID would spoil them…

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Hot Take: Josh Brolin is the best actor to have never won an Oscar.

**Score:** 1349
**Body:** I defy you to name a movie that was worse for him being part of.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Steve McQueen, Ed Norton, Willem DaFoe, Ralph Fiennes, Harvey Keitel, John Cazale, Peter O'Toole, etc.
  - Ed Norton. I mean American history X. Fucking wow
    - Primal Fear was the first thing that came to mind for me, but yeah he was great here too
  - Alan Rickman! And until very recently, Gary Oldman. That took way too long too.
    - Gary won 8 years ago!
  - came here to say Peter O'Toole. Most noms without a win too. Dude is absolutely brilliant in My Favorite Year, The Lion in Winter, Goodbye Mr. Chips, and obviously Lawrence of Arabia
2. Edward Norton
3. Peter O’Toole

Samuel L Jackson

Ralph Fiennes

Robert Redford

Kurt Russell

Tony Leung

Harrison Ford

Toshiro Mifune

Willem Dafoe

Bill Paxton

Don Cheadle

John Goodman

Roy Schieder

Tom Cruise

Robert Shaw
  - Michael Keaton???

He was nominated for Birdman, and he should have won. 

Dude was robbed.
4. Disagree - Ralph Fiennes and Jake Gyllenhaal are far better and never won one.

## 2. What was a "almost casting" that most people do not know about? I will start with Denzel as Detective Mills in "Se7en"

**Score:** 188

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Harrison Ford turned down the lead role in Schindlers List.
2. OJ Simpson was rejected as the Terminator because they thought no one would believe him as a killer 
  - Slightly related: The German dub of The Terminator used a different voice actor because Arnold is from a rural area of Austria and his German would sound "southern" in the movie. 
    - Someone else put it like this: to Germans, having Arnold's accent for a futuristic, time-traveling cyborg would be like having Larry the Cable Guy play it here in the US.
3. Tom Hanks (instead of Bill Murray) in Groundhog Day.

Will Smith (instead of Keanu Reeves) in The Matrix.
4. Finds head in box. “My man!”
5. Molly Ringwald was considered for Clarice Starling in "Silence of the Lambs"
  - "'CLARISE'? THAT'S A FAT GIRL'S NAME..." - Hannibal Lecter
6. Arnold Schwarzenegger as RoboCop
7. Tom Selleck as Indiana Jones.The producers of Magnum PI wouldn't let him do the movie.

## 3. EuroTrip is fucking hilarious.

**Score:** 142

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mi scusi
2. Berlin!
3. Here's a fun fact. YOU MADE OUT WITH YOUR SISTER, DUDE!
  - At least we learned Bratislava is the capital of Slovakia.
4. rip Michelle she was so beautiful.
5. I have been driving for 14 hours straight and I haven't slept in three days and I am wired on schnapps, benzedrine, and those little chocolate covered peanuts.
6. Nowhere

Near

Berlin 😃
7. I start my own hotel! lol
8. 🎶Scotty Doesn't Know🎶

## 4. Which of these three is Taylor Sheridan’s best screenplay?

**Score:** 119
**Body:** 1. Sicario (2015)

2. Hell or High Water (2016) 

3. Wind River (2017) 


Three absolute masterpieces in three years no other way about it. I will say Sicario but there is a solid case for any of these three. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. For me it’s Hell or High Water

“ what don’t you want with that”
  - You either don’t want the corn, or you don’t want the green beans.
2. Hell or High Water
3. Anyone who says Sicario (which imo is the best of these films) probably doesn’t realise that the screenplay changed a lot from what Sheridan originally wrote. Villeneuve and the actors, particularly Del Toro, workshopped it to make it more believable and as an example stripped back a lot of Alejandro’s dialogue. I encourage anyone who hasn’t to read the [original script](https://indiegroundfilms.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/01/sicario-by-taylor-sheridan.pdf), it’s quite different from the final movie.  

I like Wind River but I think the Hell or High Water script is probably his best.
  - “It makes me a Comanche”
    - “Boy, you’d think there were ten of me”
  - Wind River is fantastic but I chock that up to Renner and Olsens performance and the cinematography.  Screenplay is great, dont get me wrong but its not what carried the film.
4. I was going to go by which film I rewatch scenes from the most but dang I do that with all three of them. I guess the main one I always go back to in Wind River is the shootout while there's a lot more from the other films I look up from time to time.
5. Wind River is my favorite
6. Sicario, but I love the Frontier Trilogy.

## 5. Watched Tusk for the first time...

**Score:** 64
**Body:** I'm so done...

Have you seen it? If yes, what are your thoughts about it?😵‍💫

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “Shall we rescue him or idk do anything to stop this sadistic torture?”

“Nah throw a fish. He’s good.”
  - 😭🤣
2. Ending is so sad 😞
3. No movie has lived rent free in my head more. Disturbing, to say the least
4. Body horror in movies is just not for me, I’ve only seen this through Nostalgia Critic and that’s as far as I’m EVER gonna see it 

This is a complete nightmare scenario
5. My first thought when the movie ended was "WTF did I just watched?!"
  - My second thought when the movie ended still was "WTF did I just watched?!"
6. Great movie. Apparently Smith got the idea from a fake online advertisement for a room that required the lodger to dress as a walrus.
  - [This is the podcast where he "wrote" the movie](https://m.soundcloud.com/smodcast/smodcast-259-the-walrus-and?in=mburke1221%2Fsets%2Fbabble)

Still find it crazy someone funded it
7. I was increasingly horrified as the movie progressed.  And then I couldn't stop laughing.  I had a good time, I hope Kevin Smith does more horror.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The History of Day For Night: How Truffaut's film predicted the end of the “New Hollywood”

**Score:** 19
**Body:** At the same time in 1973 that the Hollywood studio system began to embrace auteurist filmmakers, Truffaut released a narrative film called Day for Night. In the film, Truffaut provides an update on the theory he helped originate more than 15 years earlier as a critic for Cahiers du Cinéma. From Truffaut’s film, a decentralized version of auteurism emerges, offering the “New Hollywood” an existential warning about the coercive effects of capitalism on artistic expression. Unfortunately, filmmakers like Michael Cimino ignored Truffaut’s advice, and in 1980, his film Heaven’s Gate, along with a handful of other costly, overindulgent films, would bring a swift end to the New Hollywood. 



To understand the intricacies of how the New Hollywood came to an end, I created a video essay examining the production history of the film Day for Night.



This summary is just a brief recap of the research I did, and I encourage you to watch my full video if this subject interests you further. Regardless, I welcome and look forward to any discussion this post elicits. 



[https://youtu.be/yeZfpN0u-YU](https://youtu.be/yeZfpN0u-YU)



### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 2. Rewatched The Tree of Life as an adult, feels like a memory

**Score:** 10
**Body:** I remember loving this so much as a teenager and people often saying this is one pretentious movie. Well, I was an emo kid at the time, so I figured maybe rewatching it now would give me a different impression.

This movie really passes the test of time for me. In fact, it's been part of my formatting years; the Jessica Chastain voice-over about being kind, being one with nature & appreciating the beauty of things, it's similar to Sam Mendes' American Beauty in worldview.

  
I think it's a great study on grief, loss, childhood trauma, parental relationships and introspection. The heavily criticized long shots of space and water add depth to your thought, the cinematography definitely evokes a certain mood of contemplation and feeling. I felt like I was in Jack's mind, his transgression was understandable and relatable. So was the mother's passivity, helplessness and resilience and even the father's cruelty, rigidity and fragile masculinity. 

  
Ultimately to me, it's about relief & serendipity through nature's beauty, love's tenderness, childhood memories and family dynamics. 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. So, funny story: I avoided watching this movie because friends of mine in college developed a cult around this movie. Yes, really. They watched it 10 times in a single semester, they were constantly bringing it to people’s houses or dorms for a “movie night,” and they encouraged people to talk about how it made them feel after it was done. It was super weird and rubbed many people, including and especially me, the wrong way.

I finally sat down and watched it a couple of months ago. It is Malick at his most Malick, which sounds like it really appeals to you and my college friends. For me, while I can appreciate the aesthetics and ambition of it, it just felt like a two hour sermon: preachy, big on messaging but nothing really clear, lacking substance. It’s the type of movie I know I don’t really respond to, I prefer narrative movies. 

I’m glad it still holds up for you, but even the distance away from my weird friends’ obsession didn’t enhance my viewing experience, it just ended with a shrug and a “that’s it…?” for me personally.
  - A few thoughts reading your comment:
- A the end of the day if it didn’t work for you that is fine. Expecting all people to connect with a piece of art is in opposition to the subjectivity and personal native of a lot of films, paintings, novels, etc. Though I deeply am moved by Tree of Life I don’t think it’s something dumb like “you don’t get it.” I am sure there are lots of films you adore that don’t work for me. Thats just how it works
- That being said this is the second post I have seen where people seem overly impacted/influenced by other people’s obsession with a film. I just don’t get it to be honest. Judge a film on its own terms. Watch it if looks like a film you’d enjoy. Who cares how other people enjoy it? It has nothing to do with the film and the people who made it. 
- I also don’t understand the preachy criticism. I cannot recall any clear “message.” It is exploring the ways of nature and grace as well as grief and loss of innocence. I didn’t come away with any direction. These are worthy topics to explore but I think it would be foolish and in opposition to what Malick is trying to do if he said this was the right and wrong way. That you find these explorations lacking substance is unfortunate but again, you are free to think that.
    - Uhh, you seem to be reading in between the lines quite a bit, I said that I took distance between what went down college and actually watching the movie because I wanted to watch it on its own terms. 

And yeah, part of my issue is that it doesn’t seem to be about anything in particular but nevertheless wants to feel important. When characters are just narrating things like “mother…. father….” it comes off vapid and self-serious. The movie is obsessed with Judeo-Christian ritualism and imagery but doesn’t seem to actually promote it, which makes it feel so sermon-like to me: Malick’s not trying to persuade new converts with this, he’s just preaching to the choir who is already invested.
    - Was going to comment but luckily read yours first. Well stated.

## 3. Does anybody else almost love reading about film more than watching it?

**Score:** 6
**Body:** I was looking at the TSPDT top 1000 and found myself more excited to read the reviews and audiences reacted to a certain film at the time of release. I ended reading about how audiences in 40s Italy and 60s Sweden reacted to bicycle thieves & persona. I found it so fascinating how an opinion of a movie can gradually shift over time from a “what the fuck is this bullshit” -> “you know this movie is actually fucking great”

I suppose I’ll end this with a question but what real time reaction about a film is most surprising to you? And how did the perception of that film change over time and why?(if it all)

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There are certainly more abstract or intellectual rather than emotional films that find a great deal more pleasure in reading and dissecting than watching - Memoria (2021) comes to mind. Can definitely relate to your post when I get the year end 'best of' Sight & Sound detailing all the releases. Or older lesser known films that I'm just never going to realistically make it to in my watchlist that are still interesting to read about their context, making and critiques of.
  - I think a lot of the times it also could be the subject matter itself. Sometimes a film has some subject matter that is just not for me. So I can make an intellectual connection, but not an emotional one. And so these films become more fun to research or analyze about than the actual viewing experience. But I can also see how I might have connected to that movie differently if I was a different person myself and was instead interested in the subject matter.
  - Another example, older activist documentaries, when the film *was* the vessel for just propagating your message and raising awareness, hard for me to weight that time value over just reading about - not to diminish the power of first hand accounting and personal narratives of course
  - Yo you just put me on I didn’t even know sight and sound did a yearly “best of” I’m gonna check that out. You’ve also given me a letterboxd list idea. “Films you find more pleasure in analyzing than watching” or something like that. And I agree with your last sentence. I’ll probably never watch every film on these lists but it’s still enjoyable to read about
2. Maybe not more, but a large part of the draw to film for me is that there’s a sort of “agreed upon” canon and like 100 years of intelligent writing and conversation about those films. 

I think things like the Sight & Sound Poll are really cool and fun to read about and think about. Same with seeing what the Great Films are in other countries. There’s a sort of Western canon for the great Chinese films but that list is often pretty different from what the Chinese critics say.
  - I definitely understand that. I find film lore to be really interesting. Typically people only focus on “old Hollywood” stories but I’m starting to become more interested in cinema stories around the globe about some of my favorite films
3. Here's my theory for why opinion of films changes over time:

When films come out, they're seen by everyone. But after a few years have passed, the general public have moved on, and they're only seen by film-lovers. The opinions of film-lovers aren't more valid than those of the general public, but they are different. So over time, the consensus shifts from the general public's opinion to the film-lovers' opinion.

Films that were too crazy for mainstream audiences gain prestige over time, while films that were too uninteresting for film-lovers lose prestige.

Does that work?
4. There’s just been a post to the sub which links to a video about Truffaut’s Day for Night. In this poster’s previous video, on Bonnie and Clyde, they quote from Pauline Kael’s review of the film: 

>Bonnie and Clyde brings into the almost frighteningly public world of movies things that people have been thinking about and saying and writing about. And once something is said or done on the screens of the world, once it has entered mass art, it can never again belong to a minority, never again be the private possession of an educated or ‘knowing’ group. But even for that group there I’d an excitement in hearing its own private thoughts expressed out loud and in seeing something of its own sensibility becoming part of our common culture.

That’s definitely an interesting quotation, an interesting thought, and an interesting piece of writing on a movie.

In answer to the question of what real time reaction to a film is the most surprising to me, one that I think about is the supposed immediate unanimous consensus that Chinatown isn’t only a good film, but that it’s a great film. There’s fifty more years of film history now. Nowadays films can get unanimously positive receptions, but I think it’s rare that there’s a unanimous reaction to a film calling it great and important, in the sense of ‘of all time’. Whereas from what I understand, it was as though that was the response to Chinatown. (I could be off about this. I believe I’m getting this impression from an extra on the film‘s DVD, as opposed to having gone back and read the reviews). Today, like I say, with too much intervening film history, I think it’s increasingly rare for critics to say, ‘this film is great’ in a way where you can rightly infer the meaning to be ‘it is one of the best movies of all time’.

## 4. Dramatic Remake of Documentaries

**Score:** 3
**Body:** I saw a trailer for “Song Sung Blue” and thought “what an interesting idea for a “songs of Neil Diamond” movie without making yet another biopic, i then found out it’s based on a documentary of the same name. 

I haven’t seen it, but i read that the Smashing Machine has re-enacted scenes and directory lifted dialog from the documentary of the same name.

There was a dramatization of “Man on Wire” called the walk that lifted the structure and visuals of the documentary

Dog Town and the Z Boys had Lords of Dog Town.

HBO made a Grey Gardens movie with directly lifted dialog and re-enacted scenes from the doc. al la Smashing Machine.

Tk a lesser extent, The Iron Claw was covered much more interestingly in a “Dark Side of the Ring” episode The Last of the Vin Erichs. 

Yes both can exist, but in most cases the film is not just covering the same story, but using the documentary as the clear sole source and inspiration. 

It’s good that a bigger movie can get these stories out, i would not have found the Song Sung Blue doc without the new movie. but at the same time they feel creatively shallow to me, usually far inferior to the documentary and at best just not needed. 

Do you know if any more examples, or any cases where the film elevates or surpasses the source documentary? 



### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Semi-related to your inquiry: the documentary Hands on a Hardbody was made into a Broadway musical. As it's a movie that's set in a small town and revolves around a truck, makes a lot of sense to have a truck in the middle of the stage. 
  - Altman was trying to develop it into a feature.
2. The much admired film **The Florida Project** clearly owes its subject matter to an HBO documentary called **Homeless: The Motel Kids of Orange County** (2010).  Mr. Baker, at least in print, has never mentioned th**is** much earlier documentary.
3. The good ones include Milk, Rescue Dawn, The Sessions and The Fighter.

Then you’ve got Marwencol becoming the Zemeckis feature Welcome to Marwen, which is a good example of why we probably don’t see this happen more often.

That said, I’m interested in what Moerten Tyldum is going to do with his adaptation of Ibelin.
  - Came to comment Rescue Dawn. It's interesting, as everything with Herzog is interesting, that Herzog fictionalized some bits of *Little Dieter Needs To Fly* and incorporated documentary elements into *Rescue Dawn.*

## 5. Title of a FNW film (that I may have hallucinated)

**Score:** 2
**Body:** I may have imagined this: a film shown at school years ago — French, New Wave, colour. My core memories have it that the shot lengths are palindromic (first cut same length as last, etc., or close), and the exact midpoint scene is in an apartment, male protagonist talking to the female protagonist. The midpoint cut is a close shot of a sliding door panel, quickly and loudly opened to reveal male protagonist. Does this ring a bell as a real film? Or have I merged a couple of films/theories? Director is non-obscure, like not Godard but someone big-namish in FNW. 

Thanks in advance for helping me play “Did I imagine this or…?”. 

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


---
